questions.question: Sound waves are classified as:
questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave — particles vibrate parallel to the direction of wave travel, and it requires a medium.

questions.question: Sound cannot travel through:
questions.explanation: Sound is a mechanical wave and requires a medium. A vacuum has no particles to transmit vibrations.

questions.question: In which of the following does sound travel fastest?
questions.explanation: Sound travels fastest in solids, then liquids, then gases. Particles in solids are closer together, transmitting vibrations more quickly.

questions.question: In a sound wave, a compression is a region where:
options: Air particles are spread furthest apart
Air pressure is lower than normal
questions.explanation: A compression is a high-pressure region where the particles of the medium are pushed closest together.

questions.question: In a sound wave, a rarefaction is a region where:
options: Air pressure is higher than normal
Air particles are spread furthest apart
questions.explanation: A rarefaction is a low-pressure region where particles are spread apart, following a compression.

questions.question: The approximate frequency range of human hearing is:
questions.explanation: The human ear can typically detect sound frequencies between $20\text{ Hz}$ and $20\text{ kHz}$.

questions.question: Sound frequencies above the range of human hearing are called:
questions.explanation: Ultrasound refers to frequencies above $20\text{ kHz}$, which are inaudible to humans but used by bats, dolphins, and in medical imaging.

questions.question: Sound frequencies below the range of human hearing are called:
questions.explanation: Infrasound refers to frequencies below $20\text{ Hz}$, which can be produced by earthquakes, elephants, and large machinery.

questions.question: Sound is classified as a mechanical wave because it:
options: Travels at the speed of light
questions.explanation: Mechanical waves need a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate. Without particles, there is no sound.

questions.question: In a sound wave traveling through air, the air particles vibrate:
options: Perpendicular to the direction of wave travel
Parallel to the direction of wave travel
Only at the source of the sound
questions.explanation: Sound is longitudinal — particles oscillate back and forth along the same line as the wave travels.

questions.question: Astronauts in space cannot hear each other directly because:
options: Sound waves travel too slowly in space
The frequency of their voices is too low
questions.explanation: Space is a near-perfect vacuum. With no particles to vibrate, sound waves cannot propagate.

questions.question: A sound wave traveling from air into water will experience:
questions.explanation: Sound travels faster in water (about $1,500\text{ m/s}$) than in air (about $340\text{ m/s}$). Particles in liquids are closer together than in gases.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the nature and properties of sound!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the difference between compression and rarefaction, and ultrasound vs. infrasound.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on why sound is a mechanical wave and how it travels through different media.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: sound as a longitudinal mechanical wave, the need for a medium, and the human hearing range.
